title: Strings
summary: Manipulate text using string methods, slicing, and formatting

sections.title: Strings are sequences of characters
sections.content: A string is a sequence of individual characters — letters, numbers, spaces, punctuation — wrapped in quotes. Think of it like a necklace where each bead is a character and the string keeps them in order. Python treats strings similarly to lists: you can access individual characters by index, slice portions, and measure length. But strings have a superpower: dozens of built-in methods for searching, modifying, and cleaning up text. Behind the scenes, every string is an object with built-in tools you can call using dot notation, like 'hello'.upper().

sections.title: Common string operations with a customer name
sections.content: This shows cleaning, formatting, and searching within a string
sections.code:
# A messy customer name with extra spaces and mixed case
customer_name = "  jOhN sMiTh  "  # Notice the spaces and odd capitalization
# strip() removes whitespace from both ends — like trimming a hedge
clean_name = customer_name.strip()  # Result: "jOhN sMiTh"
# title() capitalizes the first letter of every word
formatted_name = clean_name.title()  # Result: "John Smith"
# upper() makes everything uppercase — useful for comparisons
loud_name = clean_name.upper()  # Result: "JOHN SMITH"
# len() counts all characters including spaces
name_length = len(clean_name)  # Counts characters in "jOhN sMiTh"
# Check if a substring exists using the 'in' keyword
has_last_name = "Smith" in formatted_name  # True if found
print("Cleaned:", clean_name)
print("Formatted:", formatted_name)
print("Uppercase:", loud_name)
print("Has 'Smith':", has_last_name)
# Expected output:
# Cleaned: jOhN sMiTh
# Formatted: John Smith
# Uppercase: JOHN SMITH
# Has 'Smith': True

sections.title: String formatting with f-strings
sections.content: f-strings (formatted string literals) are the modern way to embed variables inside text. You put an 'f' before the opening quote and wrap variables in curly braces {} — Python replaces the braces with the variable's value automatically. This is much cleaner than old-style concatenation with +. For example, f"Hello, {name}! You are {age} years old." produces a complete sentence. You can even put expressions inside the braces: f"Total: ${price * quantity:.2f}" calculates and formats the result.

sections.title: Strings are immutable — you can't change them directly
sections.content: Many beginners try to modify a character directly, like name[0] = 'K'. Wrong — strings are immutable (unchangeable). Python raises a TypeError. Correct: create a new string using methods or slicing: name = 'K' + name[1:]. Methods like replace() and upper() return new strings — they don't alter the original.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: A registration system captured a user's email as '  ALEX@EXAMPLE.COM  '. Write code to trim the spaces, convert it to lowercase, and check if '@' is present. Then print a friendly welcome message using an f-string: 'Welcome, alex@example.com!'
hints: Which method removes extra whitespace from both ends of a string?
How do you convert all letters to lowercase?
